sections.title: 8.1. Treatment of strictures in trans men
sections.text: In trans men, stricture treatment depends on the time after neophallic reconstruction, stricture location, stricture length and quality of local tissues [154, 530].

sections.title: 8.1.1. Management of strictures early after neophallic reconstruction
sections.text: Urethral surgery on tissues in the acute phase of inflammation and wound healing is not indicated and should be postponed until any healing problems of the neophallus have been resolved and scar tissue formation in the urethra has been stabilised. This usually takes six months [530]. Endoscopic incision for short (< 3cm) urethral strictures has been performed, mainly at the anastomotic site, with a maximum stricture-free rate of only 16.7% when performed within six months after neophallic reconstruction [531]. Insertion of a suprapubic catheter is the first-line treatment in cases of obstructive symptoms severely affecting the patient’s QoL, recurrent UTI or retention. The alternative is perineostomy, which is a specialist procedure and should be performed by a urologist familiar with transgender urethral anatomy. The perineostomy may be closed at the time of formal urethral reconstruction [532].

sections.title: 8.1.2. Treatment of meatal stenosis in trans men
sections.text: Intermittent urethral dilatation is an option as palliative treatment for low-grade meatal stenosis [24]. Patients with high-grade meatal stenosis, those who refuse ISD, or those who want a durable solution should be offered simple meatotomy. Patency rates are 50% and 75% for transmen after, respectively, metoidioplasty and phalloplasty [154]. The drawback is that the meatus will be in a hypospadiac position. Alternatively, a staged urethroplasty can be offered [154].

sections.title: 8.1.3. Treatment of strictures at the neophallic urethra
sections.text: The standard treatment for these strictures is staged urethroplasty, with or without graft augmentation (BMG or full thickness SG). A patency rate of 50–88% [154, 530, 533] has been reported after phalloplasty and up to 100% after metoidioplasty [154, 530]. For complex (e.g. fully obliterated) or recurrent strictures at the neophallic urethra, a complete urethral substitution with a tubularised radial forearm-free flap has been proposed with a 67% patency rate [154].

sections.title: 8.1.4. Treatment of strictures at the anastomosis neophallic urethra-fixed part of the urethra
sections.text: Short non-obliterative strictures can be treated by endoscopic incision. A first endoscopic incision has a 37–45.5% patency rate, but this dropped to 0% in case of three or more attempts (median follow-up of 51 months) [530, 531]. Therefore, repetitive endoscopic incisions should be discouraged unless with palliative intent. For very short (< 1.5cm) low-grade strictures, Heineke-Mikulicz urethroplasty is an option reporting a 58–80% after phalloplasty and up to 100% after metoidioplasty [154, 530]. If the stricture is nearly or completely obliterative, options are EPA, graft augmentation urethroplasty or staged urethroplasty. Excision and primary anastomosis yields a patency rate of 46–57% after phalloplasty and 78% after metoidioplasty [154, 530]. Alternatively, or if EPA is not possible (stricture length > 2cm), a graft augmentation urethroplasty can be performed with a 56–100% patency rate [154, 530]. In case of insufficient ventral tissue during graft urethroplasty, it is advised to support this graft by a local fasciocutaneous flap [154]. An alternative (especially after failure of the previous techniques) can be a staged approach [154, 530].

sections.title: 8.1.5. Treatment of strictures at the fixed part of the urethra
sections.text: This part of the urethra has a more reliable blood supply, and the dorsal part of the urethra is supported by the corporal bodies of the clitoris. Therefore, single-stage dorsal inlay graft urethroplasty is possible for strictures at this site, especially after metoidioplasty, with up to 100% patency rate [530]. Staged repair with or without a dorsal graft is an alternative for these rare strictures [530].

sections.title: 8.1.6. Definitive perineostomy in trans men
sections.text: Definitive perineostomy should be offered to patients with refractory strictures or to those with strictures who do not wish to have complex reconstructive surgery [154, 530].

sections.title: 8.2. Perioperative care after treatment of strictures in trans men
sections.text: Anecdotally, after endoscopic incision and urethroplasty, the urethral catheter is maintained for two to three weeks [531, 534]. Pericatheter urethrography is advised before catheter removal as it might be challenging to reinsert the urethral catheter in case of urinary extravasation [534].

sections.title: 8.3. Strictures in trans women
sections.text: It is acceptable to start with dilation of a short and non-obliterative stricture in trans women, although no long-term data about the effectiveness are available [24]. If this is not possible, or if it fails, a short (< 1cm) meatal stricture can be treated by Y-V meatoplasty with an 85% stricture-free rate [535]. Somewhat longer (1–2cm) meatal strictures can be treated by a neovaginal advancement flap (inverted U or '7-flap') with no recurrence observed after 37 months median follow-up [536]. Summary of evidence LE After neophallic reconstruction, local tissues go through the various stages of wound healing, and stable wound healing is usually achieved after six months. 3 After two attempts, endoscopic incision is no longer successful in trans men. 3 Two-stage urethroplasty for strictures at the neophallic urethra has a patency rate of 50–88% after phalloplasty and up to 100% after metoidioplasty. 3 Y-V meatoplasty for short (< 1cm) meatal stenosis in trans women has a stricture-free rate of 85%. 3 Recommendations Strength rating Do not perform endoscopic incision or urethroplasty within six months after neophalloplasty. Strong Do not perform more than two endoscopic incisions for strictures in trans men unless with palliative intent. Strong Perform staged urethroplasty for strictures at the neophallic urethra if open reconstruction is indicated. Weak Perform Y-V meatoplasty for short (< 1cm) meatal stenosis in trans women if open reconstruction is indicated. Weak

rows: After neophallic reconstruction, local tissues go through the various stages of wound healing, and stable wound healing is usually achieved after six months.
After two attempts, endoscopic incision is no longer successful in trans men.
Two-stage urethroplasty for strictures at the neophallic urethra has a patency rate of 50–88% after phalloplasty and up to 100% after metoidioplasty.
Y-V meatoplasty for short (< 1cm) meatal stenosis in trans women has a stricture-free rate of 85%.
Do not perform endoscopic incision or urethroplasty within six months after neophalloplasty.
Do not perform more than two endoscopic incisions for strictures in trans men unless with palliative intent.
Perform staged urethroplasty for strictures at the neophallic urethra if open reconstruction is indicated.
Perform Y-V meatoplasty for short (< 1cm) meatal stenosis in trans women if open reconstruction is indicated.
